title: Turbulent Waters
description: In the shadow of Idaho's biggest engineering project, Jake and Anna are on opposite sides of the river—until the current pulls them together and the flood takes everything else.
body:
> Jake Ford is proud of his construction background. The family's construction company has existed for three generations, but when he returns to Idaho with his engineering degree, there is no place for him in the family business. He takes a job as an assistant engineer on the construction of the Teton Dam, a massive earthen dam being built to irrigate 11,000 acres of arid Idaho farmland. Anna Stone is an environmentalist opposed to the construction of the dam, fearing it will destroy the habitat of the Yellowstone Cutthroat trout, which her family relies on for their fishing guide business. When Jake and Anna meet on the river one evening while flyfishing, the attraction is immediate. Their future looks bright until they each discover the other's connection to the dam. They go their separate ways, but fate draws them back together despite their turbulent relationship. When it looks like they might find a workable friendship, the unthinkable happens. On June 5, 1976, the Teton Dam fails catostrophically. Anna doesn't know what happened to Jake, other than she saw his pickup truck swept downstream on a television news cast. Can they navigate the turbulent floodwaters and find one another?

## The Real History of the Teton Dam Collapse

On June 5, 1976, the earthen Teton Dam failed catastrophically. The breach sent 80 billion gallons of water tearing through the Upper Snake River Valley, destroying homes, businesses, and livelihoods. *Turbulent Waters* places readers directly in the path of this 1976 Idaho flood disaster. The narrative strips away the sterile statistics of the event to focus on the human cost. It captures the sudden devastation of the floodwaters and the long, difficult aftermath faced by the communities of eastern Idaho.

## Fly Fishing and the Environmental Conflict

The Teton River provides the central battleground for the novel. Anna Stone represents a growing environmental movement in the 1970s, fighting to protect the fragile Yellowstone Cutthroat trout habitat. The conflict reflects real historical tensions in the region. The narrative details the local fly fishing culture, contrasting the quiet rhythm of the river with the heavy machinery reshaping the canyon. The river brings Jake and Anna together, and it is the resource that ultimately tears their worlds apart.

## Engineering an Idaho Disaster

Jake Ford's role on the project highlights the technical ambitions of the era. The massive dam was designed to secure reliable irrigation for 11,000 acres of arid Idaho farmland. The novel explores the mechanics of earthen dam construction and the immense pressure placed on the engineering teams. By examining the disaster through the eyes of an assistant engineer, the story asks difficult questions about human hubris, the limits of infrastructure, and the consequences of attempting to control nature.

seo.metaDescription: In the shadow of Idaho's biggest engineering project, Jake and Anna are on opposite sides of the river—until the current pulls them together and the flood takes everything else.
